name: The Off Set
classification_notes: A bundled collection of 16 typefaces spanning display, experimental, serif, sans-serif and decorative styles; categorised as display given the overtly expressive, graphic-led character of the majority of included fonts.
summary: A bundled collection of all 16 Off Type typefaces, covering 199 styles across experimental, display, serif and sans-serif designs, sold for $15 for personal use.
description: The Off Set packages every typeface Off Type have released into a single purchase, adding 79 styles not available in the free trial downloads. The collection ranges from the LCD-inspired Sector and the distorted Didone Weird Serif to the condensed Neue Montreal Squeezed series and the musical Rhapsody. Ten Photoshop mockup templates by Bendito Mockups are included alongside OTF, TTF, WOFF and WOFF2 formats.
foundry_blurb: Off Type describe The Off Set as the fastest way to upgrade your design toolkit, bundling all 16 of their typefaces and 199 styles together with ten pro mockup templates by Bendito Mockups. They position it as suitable for both seasoned designers and newcomers, and note that fonts in the pack are for personal use only, with commercial licences available separately from $30.

name: Spaceship
classification_notes: A single-weight display face with expressive, organic letterforms and sharp terminals, drawing on psychedelic typography and Art Nouveau; firmly in the display category with no text-setting intent.
summary: A retro-futuristic display face by Lucie Giriat and Mat Desjardins, combining organic curves, sharp terminals and Art Nouveau influences drawn from the golden age of space exploration.
description: A single-weight display typeface inspired by mid-century sci-fi cinema and psychedelic graphic design, Spaceship merges unconventional letterform proportions with flowing, spike-edged strokes. The 406-glyph set covers Latin Extended A for broad Western language support, and an alternate Y is available via OpenType. Best suited to large-scale headlines, poster work, album covers and branding where personality takes precedence over neutrality.
foundry_blurb: Off Type describe Spaceship as a retro-futuristic display typeface inspired by the golden age of space exploration, sci-fi cinema and visionary graphic design. They note it balances organic curves with unconventional proportions, merging psychedelic typography, Art Nouveau influences and cosmic aesthetics, and that it transforms words into graphic objects readable at large sizes.

name: Sector
classification_notes: Built on a fixed grid inspired by retro LCD segmented displays, Sector is a geometric display face with a strict modular construction; its playful stencil-like forms and optional COLR/grid overlays place it firmly in the display category.
summary: A grid-locked display typeface by Valerio Monopoli built on the logic of retro LCD segmented displays, spanning 12 styles across normal and narrow widths.
description: Every glyph in the family derives from a single fixed grid module, a constraint that channels the segmented logic of retro LCD screens into a typeface capable of 700 glyphs per style, including diacritics, small caps, alternates and fractions. The family splits into Sector and the sharper-terminated Sector Narrow, each running Light, Regular and Bold weights alongside three matching Rotalic styles that introduce slant without breaking the grid. Styles ship with Base, COLR colour and blueprint grid variants, making it equally serviceable as a bold headline stencil or an unexpectedly readable text face.
foundry_blurb: Off Type describe Sector as inspired by the logic of retro LCD displays, where simple segmented systems express complex alphabets. Designed by Valerio Monopoli on a single uncompromising grid module, the collection covers 700 glyphs per style across 12 styles, two condensations and Rotalic variants, positioning it as the most versatile typeface in its admittedly narrow genre.

name: Weird Serif
classification_notes: A Didone-derived display serif with dramatic stroke contrast and a large x-height, but deliberately distorted with unusual hanging and connecting serifs that push it firmly into expressive display territory rather than conventional high-contrast text use.
summary: A deliberately distorted take on the Didone genre by Alex Slobzheninov, replacing fashion-world solemnity with unusual hanging serifs and long connecting strokes.
description: Built on the high-contrast skeleton of Didone typefaces common in fashion publishing, it subverts the genre with idiosyncratic downward-hanging serifs on letters like V, W and K, and long connecting serifs that can be toggled via Style Set 1. Twelve cuts span six weights from Thin to Black with matching italics, and a variable font is included. The large x-height and punchy contrast suit display headlines where something more irreverent than a straight Bodoni revival is wanted.
foundry_blurb: Off Type describe Weird Serif as a freaky take on the fancy Didone typefaces commonly used in the fashion industry, keeping the drama but ditching the seriousness. Weird hanging serifs and long connecting serifs distinguish it from its source genre, with the serifs optionally controlled via Style Set 1. Available in 12 styles plus a variable cut, with 408 glyphs each.

name: Jubilee
classification_notes: Jubilee spans sans to serif across its six subfamilies, but its conceptual lineage from Gill Sans places it in the humanist sans tradition; the graduated 'serifness' across subfamilies makes strict categorisation difficult, though the sans end of the spectrum anchors it here.
summary: A 84-style family by Valerio Monopoli drawing on Gill Sans and British royal iconography, graduated across six subfamilies from sans to serif.
description: Designed by Valerio Monopoli with type design collaboration from Piero Beninato, Jubilee takes Gill Sans as a ghostly reference point and grafts onto it the visual codes of British royal ceremony. The six subfamilies — Silver through Platinum — progress along an axis of increasing serifness, echoing the Jubilees of Queen Elizabeth II's reign. Deliberately uneven proportions and an extended glyph set including royal crests and gem-like punctuation give it a hand-crafted quality that separates it from more clinical humanist designs.
foundry_blurb: Off Type describe Jubilee as a bold reimagining of tradition, fusing the regal pomp of the Windsor Royal Family with the ghost of Gill Sans to forge something defiantly modern. The six subfamilies stretch from Silver to Platinum across both serifness and optical size, mirroring each of Queen Elizabeth II's Jubilees. An intricate glyph set featuring royal icons and gem-like punctuation completes what they call a crown jewel in any designer's toolkit.

name: Pergamon
classification_notes: Pergamon draws on parchment nib contrast and gestural calligraphic strokes, placing it in the tradition of digital calligraphic serif types; its structured weight range and Greek support give it versatility beyond purely expressive scripts.
summary: A calligraphic serif by Valerio Monopoli that reworks parchment nib contrast into a structured 16-style family, offered in Smooth and Rough sub-families.
description: Named after the city where parchment was invented, it takes the stroke contrast of a broad-nib pen and formalises it across eight weights from Extralight to Black in both a clean Smooth cut and a textured Rough variant. The family ships with a variable font and includes monotonic Greek support, making it one of the few typefaces in the Off Type catalogue to do so. Aimed at digital editorial work, it sits between expressive calligraphic display type and a more structured text face.
foundry_blurb: Off Type describe Pergamon as the spiritual heir to one of the most recognisable and divisive digital calligraphic fonts, named after the ancient city credited with inventing parchment. They say it brings parchment nib contrast into a structured and versatile typeface, balancing organic gestural strokes with digital precision, and note it as the first typeface on Off Type or Pangram Pangram to include monotonic Greek compatibility.

name: Bulb
classification_notes: Bulb is a purely expressive display type family drawing on the visual language of light bulb glows and old punch printers; its three sub-families, including a reverse-contrast cut, place it firmly in decorative display territory rather than any functional classification.
summary: A 27-style display family by Mat Desjardins built around the visual metaphor of light bulb glow, with a Watt-based weight axis running from dim to brilliantly blurred.
description: Three sub-families — Classic, Reverse and Monoline — each span nine weights from 100 to 900 Watt, where heavier settings introduce a deliberately bloated, light-leak quality. Bulb Classic carries conventional stroke contrast, Bulb Reverse flips it, and Bulb Monoline strips it away entirely for a cleaner, uniform stroke. Variable cuts accompany each sub-family, and each style includes a set of icons and glyphs tied to the lighting theme.
foundry_blurb: Off Type describe Bulb as infusing the timeless charm of old punch printers with the brilliance of light bulbs, capturing the allure of light leaks as they overlap, grow and collide in constellations. Rather than a traditional weight axis, Bulb uses a Watt-based axis from 100 — its dimmest glow — to 900, where letterforms become blurry and brilliant. The family comprises three distinct sub-families: Bulb Classic, Bulb Reverse and Bulb Monoline, each adorned with icons and glyphs.

designer: Mat Desjardins, Francesca Bolognini, Fred Wiltshire
classification_notes: An angular, glitch-inflected display sans with distorted strokes and fragmented forms; the deliberate digital-anomaly aesthetic places it firmly in the experimental display category rather than any conventional sans classification.
summary: A nine-weight angular display typeface built around deliberate glitch aesthetics, with warped strokes and fragmented forms framed as the output of an AI making errors.
description: Designed by Mat Desjardins, Francesca Bolognini and Fred Wiltshire, the family spans Thin to Black with a variable cut and 455 glyphs covering Latin Extended A. Each weight carries intentional distortions: strokes warp, lines twist and shapes fragment to suggest digital malfunction. The concept is built around the question of whether a machine or a human is ultimately responsible for the forms, giving it a narrative hook useful for sci-fi, tech and editorial contexts.
foundry_blurb: Off Type describe 2049 as born from the chaotic dance between human intention and artificial intelligence's persistent error, with each character embodying the struggle of an AI creating order from chaos. They position the typeface as a challenge to conventional uniformity, inviting designers to explore digital anomalies, and leave open the question of whether it was genuinely made by artificial intelligence.

name: Neue Montreal Squeezed Collection
classification_notes: Described explicitly as a Grotesque sans-serif, derived from Neue Montreal; the squeezed widths are compressed variants of that grotesque base rather than a distinct classification shift.
summary: A family of four compressed widths derived from Neue Montreal, spanning Semi Squeezed to Ultra Squeezed across 56 styles and variable cuts.
description: Built directly on the Neue Montreal grotesque, this collection replaces the chore of manually compressing type with four professionally drawn widths, each carrying seven weights from Thin to Bold with matching italics. The family runs to 56 static styles plus variable cuts, covering Latin Extended A and Cyrillic. Aimed squarely at expressive graphic-design applications where tight, tall settings are the point rather than a workaround.
foundry_blurb: Off Type position the collection as a time-saving alternative to manually compressing fonts, describing Neue Montreal itself as an incredibly popular, hard-working, timeless grotesque sans-serif. The squeezed counterparts are pitched as trend-ready extensions of that workhorse, designed by Mat Desjardins and Valerio Monopoli with visuals by Sergio Lairisa and text by Studio Ground Floor.

name: Abalos
classification_notes: A display-oriented sans with cursive and swash features, drawing on retro-futuristic and loosely Arabic-influenced curves. Its expressive, wavy letterforms and speculative aesthetic place it firmly in the display category.
summary: A retro-futuristic display sans by Valerio Monopoli, named after a Martian sand dune and shaped by loosely Arabic-inflected curves.
description: Nine weights from Hairline to Extrabold, each carrying swash terminals and optional cursive features that nod to Middle Eastern letterforms without directly quoting them. The concept frames the typeface as a speculative aesthetic for human life on Mars, bridging desert topologies of the past with science-fiction futures. OpenType features include swash terminals, italic shapes and an uncial A, giving designers room to modulate its expressiveness.
foundry_blurb: Off Type describe Abalos as serene, spacey and sleek, designed against the backdrop of humanity's next big adventure: Mars exploration. Named after one of Mars' sand dunes, its letterforms draw undulating influence from desert-like waves of Middle Eastern topology, with restrained Arabic references visible in its cursive features. The typeface aims to find fresh footing in the combination of the past and a speculative future.

name: OT Brut
classification_notes: A high-contrast serif in the Bodoni/Didone tradition, but stripped of curves entirely in favour of angular, constructed letterforms that the foundry explicitly frames as a brutalist reimagining of neoclassical models.
summary: A curveless, angular reworking of Bodoni by Valerio Monopoli, trading neoclassical elegance for rigid, brutalist construction across four styles including mono variants.
description: Where Bodoni relies on the tension between hairlines and thick strokes modulated by curves, OT Brut replaces all curves with straight angles, producing a high-contrast serif that reads as architectural rather than fashionable. Four styles cover proportional and monospaced versions in regular and italic, each carrying 502 glyphs with discretionary ligatures designed to interlock and compose. The mono styles also include coding abbreviations, making the family useful across editorial, poster and interface contexts.
foundry_blurb: Off Type describe OT Brut as a brutalist, curveless reimagining of Bodoni that flips the genre-defining neoclassical classic on its head, replacing its traditional drops with angular, constructed forms suited for contexts beyond the runway. The family includes discretionary ligatures and mono styles with coding abbreviations, designed to help create interlocking typographic compositions.

name: Miniature
classification_notes: Described by the foundry as having a sturdy baroque structure with an unconventionally tall x-height; the flamboyant detailing, theatrical proportions and display orientation place it firmly in baroque display serif territory.
summary: A baroque display serif with an unusually tall x-height, balancing theatrical flair with functional restraint across 14 styles and a variable axis.
description: Designed by Valerio Monopoli with type design collaboration from Andrea Biggio, Miniature draws on baroque structure while pushing the x-height well beyond convention, giving it a distinctive vertical tension. The family spans seven upright and seven italic styles from Extralight to Black, available as static OTF/TTF or as variable fonts. Off Type position it for punchy postmodern brands, editorial work and contemporary cultural exhibition contexts.
foundry_blurb: Off Type describe Miniature as welcoming both the theatrical and the restrained, balancing the two through a sturdy baroque structure and an unconventionally tall x-height. They call it an earnest display font suited to punchy postmodern brands, editorial explorations and contemporary cultural exhibitions, with 14 styles across seven weights plus a variable cut and 521 glyphs covering Latin Extended A.

name: Négligé
classification_notes: A high-contrast serif with geometric underpinnings, decorative swash capitals and stencil variants; the dramatic thick-thin stroke contrast and ornamental features place it firmly in the display category.
summary: A high-contrast display serif by Valerio Monopoli that mixes geometric structure with swash capitals, stencil cuts and a variable font across five optical sizes.
description: Designed by Valerio Monopoli, the family spans five named sizes from XX Small to Large, each available in both a regular and stencil version, plus a variable cut. Swash capitals, curly alternates, discretionary ligatures and old-style figures sit alongside the contrasting strokes, giving it range for fashion, editorial and identity work. The stencil subfamily adds an alternative register without departing from the overall character.
foundry_blurb: Off Type describe Négligé as existing between the worlds of geometry and the decoratively eccentric, questioning what it means to create an erotic experience through type. The typeface challenges conventional skeleton structure by shifting between plunging curves, elegant ligatures and razor-sharp cuts, with swash capitals and curly alternatives available across regular, stencil and variable formats.

name: Missouri
classification_notes: A chunky, wide-proportion bold grotesque designed explicitly for large-scale display use, with aperture-inspired ink traps and broad cinemascope proportions that place it firmly in the display category.
summary: A wide, chunky display grotesque by Valerio Monopoli, named after his local cinema and built around broad cinemascope proportions with aperture-inspired ink traps.
description: Four weights run from 8mm to 70mm, each carrying the same 489-glyph set and available as a variable font. The design translates the wide-screen ratio of cinema into squat, bold letterforms punctuated with ink traps that echo film-reel sprockets. Best suited to posters, titles and branding where the letterforms can be set at size.
foundry_blurb: Off Type describe Missouri as a lasting dedication to cinema, with its name taken from the designer's local picture house. They say it translates the wide angles of watching a film into chunky, broad grotesque proportions, with aperture-inspired ink traps akin to film-reel sprockets, and intend it to be projected as large as possible.

name: Rhapsody
classification_notes: Rhapsody draws explicitly from Didot's typographic structure, reinterpreting the high-contrast, rational skeleton of the didone tradition through a modern, music-influenced lens suited to display use.
summary: A Didot-inspired display face by Valerio Monopoli, shaped by research linking musical notation to didone typography and packed with musical glyphs and discretionary ligatures.
description: Born from Reading University research that traced the design of contemporary musical notation back to Didot's typographic rationale, this eight-weight family reworks those high-contrast letterforms for a musical context. The glyph set includes dedicated musical notation characters alongside lyrical discretionary ligatures, old-style figures and italic shape alternates. At 568 glyphs per style, it is an unusually considered display face for editorial, album and concert-adjacent work.
foundry_blurb: Off Type describe Rhapsody as emerging from Reading University research which found that modern musical notation was inspired by the typographic structure of Didot. Designer Valerio Monopoli used that connection to build a Didot-like exploration of contemporary letterforms in a melodic context, resulting in what they call a graceful, fluid font defined by a musical mashup of orchestra-inspired profiles and minimalist decadence.

name: Yokai
classification_notes: Described explicitly as an extended grotesque by the foundry, with broad, semi-fixed-width letterforms, low x-height and a brutalist character that places it firmly in the grotesque sans-serif tradition.
summary: A broad, low-x-height extended grotesque with brutalist and futuristic leanings, built around semi-fixed-width letterforms and mirrored uppercase alternates.
description: Designed by Valerio Monopoli, Yokai is an extended grotesque with a deliberately squat proportion and low x-height that gives it an otherworldly, compressed presence. The family spans seven weights from Extralight to Extrabold, each containing 590 glyphs, and is available as a variable font. OpenType features include mirrored uppercase alternates, discretionary ligatures, ring-shaped dots and old-style figures, lending it flexibility for display and branding work where character is a priority.
foundry_blurb: Off Type describe Yokai as named after the Japanese word for mysterious, ghostly apparitions, calling it a monster that is more creature comfort than creature feature. They characterise it as short and squat, with a semi-fixed width and low x-height that creates a mythical mixture of brutalist and futuristic qualities, rounded out with discretionary ligatures and mirrored uppercase alternates.

name: Rayuela
classification_notes: A tall, condensed stencil-like display face with semi-gothic characteristics and a structural stacking system; purpose-built for large-scale display rather than text use.
summary: A condensed stencil display face by Valerio Monopoli with a built-in stacking system that lets characters be layered in pairs using contextual OpenType logic.
description: Named after Julio Cortázar's novel and inspired by the children's game of hopscotch, the typeface uses a rhythmic semi-gothic structure in which uppercase and lowercase characters can stack vertically through OpenType contextual alternates. A single weight ships with 525 glyphs covering Latin Extended A, alongside features including old-style figures, single-storey alternates and fractions. Suited to posters, packaging and any display context where stacked or layered lettering is the primary effect.
foundry_blurb: Off Type describe Rayuela as a condensed stencil-like semi-gothic typeface inspired by the classic playground game of hopscotch, taking its name from the Julio Cortázar novella. Its characters are contextually aware and stackable in pairs, available at full size, half size or layered, and designed for display applications from small text boxes to large-scale typographic compositions.
